questions.question: A 32-year-old male is evaluated for a left-to-right shunt. CT demonstrates a right-sided pulmonary vein draining oxygenated blood into the SVC. Which congenital heart defect is most classically associated with this finding?
questions.detail: PAPVR (especially of the RUL) is strongly associated with sinus venosus type ASDs.

questions.question: Which of the following defines Partial Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Return (PAPVR)?
choices: All pulmonary veins drain into the right atrium.
One or more pulmonary veins drain into the systemic venous system.
A pulmonary artery arises from the aorta.
Pulmonary veins drain into a left-sided SVC.
Oxygenated blood from the left atrium enters the right atrium.
questions.detail: This is the definition of PAPVR.

questions.question: "Scimitar Syndrome" is a specific form of PAPVR characterized by which of the following triads?
choices: Left-sided PAPVR, pulmonary sequestration, and left arch.
Right inferior pulmonary vein draining into the IVC, unilateral pulmonary hypoplasia, and right-sided heart.
Common pulmonary vein ostia, ASD, and pulmonary hypertension.
Right superior pulmonary vein draining into the SVC, azygos continuation, and polysplenia.
questions.detail: This describes the Scimitar sign and associated syndrome.

questions.question: In "Meandering Pulmonary Vein," what is the critical anatomical detail that differentiates it from PAPVR?
choices: It causes a massive left-to-right shunt.
It drains oxygenated blood into the IVC.
It follows an anomalous course but always returns to the left atrium.
It is associated with right heart failure.
It is only found in the upper lobes.
questions.detail: Unlike PAPVR, meandering veins do NOT cause a shunt because they return to the LA.

questions.question: A 55-year-old female with a history of severe mitral regurgitation presents with a dilated right inferior pulmonary vein on CT. This finding is termed a:
questions.detail: Enlarged pulmonary veins are typically called a varix.

questions.question: Mitral regurgitation is a known cause of which asymmetric pulmonary finding?
questions.detail: Mitral regurgitation classically causes asymmetric RUL edema.

questions.question: "Proximal Interruption of the Pulmonary Artery" most commonly occurs on which side relative to the aortic arch?
choices: On the same side as the arch
On the opposite side of the arch
It only involves the left side
It is unrelated to the arch position
questions.detail: Side of interruption is determined by the side of the arch.

questions.question: Interruption of the LEFT pulmonary artery is classically associated with which condition?
questions.detail: Interrupted left PA is specifically associated with TOF and Truncus.

questions.question: On a frontal chest radiograph, regional oligemia (decreased vascular markings) distal to a pulmonary embolism is known as:
questions.detail: This represents regional oligemia on CXR.

questions.question: A peripheral wedge-shaped opacity on CXR in the setting of pulmonary embolism represents:
questions.detail: This is a peripheral wedge-shaped opacity.

questions.question: When assessing for right heart strain on CT, what is considered a highly reliable sign?
choices: Leftward bowing of the interventricular septum
Contrast reflux into the azygos vein
The Right Ventricle (RV) being larger than the Left Ventricle (LV)
questions.detail: Probably the best CT sign of right heart strain.

questions.question: "Chronic" Pulmonary Embolism is characterized on CT by which of the following?
choices: Peripheral, web-like clots and shrunken veins with collaterals
Sudden right heart failure in a previously healthy patient
questions.detail: These are features of chronic PE.

questions.question: Which imaging modality is described in the text as "probably superior to CT" for the evaluation of chronic pulmonary embolism?
questions.detail: V/Q is described as "probably superior to CT" for chronic PE.

questions.question: A patient with Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension (PAH) has a normal pulmonary capillary wedge pressure. This combination should raise suspicion for:
questions.detail: PAH + Normal Wedge pressure should suggest PVOD.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of an iatrogenic pulmonary artery pseudoaneurysm?
questions.detail: This is the most common cause of PA pseudoaneurysm.

questions.question: The "Rasmussen Aneurysm" is a pulmonary artery pseudoaneurysm associated with:
questions.detail: Rasmussen aneurysm is a PA pseudoaneurysm secondary to TB.

questions.question: Hughes-Stovin Syndrome is a rare condition characterized by:
choices: Recurrent thrombophlebitis and pulmonary artery aneurysm rupture
questions.detail: This defines Hughes-Stovin.

questions.question: In the evaluation of pulmonary hypertension, what pressure threshold (in mmHg) is used for the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Pressures over 25 mmHg make the diagnosis of pulmonary hypertension.

questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with primary pulmonary hypertension. According to the text, this demographic is:
questions.detail: Primary idiopathic PAH is very uncommon.

questions.question: In COPD patients, a "worse outcome" is predicted if the Main Pulmonary Artery is:
questions.detail: COPDers with a PA/A ratio > 1 have increased mortality.

questions.question: The "Banana and Egg" sign for identifying a big pulmonary artery on CT involves seeing:
choices: The Main PA at the level of the arch
The right PA crossing the carina
The Left PA hooking over the bronchus
questions.detail: This morphologic sign suggests PAH.

questions.question: "Carina Crossover" refers to which finding in pulmonary hypertension?
choices: The left PA crossing behind the trachea
The right PA crossing the carina midline anteriorly
The Main PA crossing the aortic arch
Retrograde flow in the bronchial arteries
Enlarged hilar nodes splaying the carina
questions.detail: In PAH, the right PA crosses more cranially than normal.

questions.question: Total Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Return (TAPVR) Type 1 is "Supracardiac" and is associated with which radiographic sign?
questions.detail: TAPVR Type 1 (Supracardiac) drains above the heart, creating this look.

questions.question: TAPVR Type 3 (Infracardiac) is unique because it often presents in the newborn with:
choices: A very large heart and clear lungs
A full-on pulmonary edema look due to venous obstruction
questions.detail: Type 3 drains below the diaphragm and is often obstructed.

questions.question: For the survival of a patient with TAPVR, which of the following is REQUIRED?
questions.detail: Admixture is required for survival in TAPVR.

questions.question: In Tc-99m MAA VQ scanning, what is the significance of seeing "tracer in the brain"?
questions.detail: Tracer in the brain signifies a shunt (ASD, VSD, or AVM).

questions.question: What is the standard size of Tc-99m MAA particles used for perfusion imaging?
questions.detail: Particles must be larger than capillaries (10um) but smaller than arterioles (150um).

questions.question: In which of the following patients should the number of MAA particles be REDUCED?
questions.detail: Particles are reduced in those with fewer capillaries.

questions.question: If a technician accidentally draws blood into the syringe prior to a Tc-99m MAA injection, what will be seen on the scan?
choices: Clumped MAA (multiple focal hot spots in the lungs)
questions.detail: Blood in the syringe prior to injection causes clumping/hot spots.

questions.question: On a Xenon-133 ventilation scan, "persistent pulmonary activity during washout" is a sign of:
questions.detail: Xenon retention during washout indicates air trapping.

questions.question: Xenon-133 accumulation in the Right Upper Quadrant (RUQ) during a VQ scan represents:
questions.detail: Xenon is fat-soluble and can accumulate in a fatty liver.

questions.question: A "unilateral perfusion defect" of an entire lung with a normal ventilation scan is most commonly caused by:
choices: A central obstructing mass (e.g., bronchogenic carcinoma)
questions.detail: This is the most common cause of a unilateral whole-lung perfusion defect.

questions.question: In the setting of a known right-to-left shunt, how many MAA particles should typically be administered?
questions.detail: Standard 500K particles should be reduced to 100K if a shunt is known.

questions.question: For a neonate undergoing a VQ scan, what particle reduction is recommended?
questions.detail: Neonates have far fewer capillaries and need significant reduction.

questions.question: If you see tracer in the thyroid or stomach during a VQ scan, what are the two main considerations?
choices: Massive PE or Right heart strain
Free Tc or a Right-to-left shunt
questions.detail: These are the two main reasons for tracer in the thyroid/stomach.

questions.question: Diaphragmatic injury in trauma is three times more common on which side?
choices: It depends on the age of the patient
It only involves the central tendon
questions.detail: The liver acts as a buffer on the right side.

questions.question: The "Collar Sign" in diaphragmatic injury represents:
choices: A waist-like appearance of a herniated organ through the diaphragm
Air extending above the great vessels
Deviation of the trachea to the right
questions.detail: The "Collar Sign" is the waist-like appearance of herniated organs.

questions.question: Which sign describes the absence of lung tissue between the chest wall and upper abdominal organs (liver/stomach) in diaphragmatic injury?
questions.detail: This is the absence of lung between the chest wall and abdominal organs.

questions.question: Tracheo-bronchial injury most commonly occurs within how many centimeters of the carina?
questions.detail: Airway injury usually occurs within 2 cm of the carina.

questions.question: Injury close to the carina is more likely to cause which of the following?
questions.detail: Injury close to the carina causes pneumomediastinum rather than pneumothorax.

questions.question: Tracheal lacerations most commonly occur at which anatomical junction?
choices: The vocal cords and subglottic space
The junction of the cartilaginous and membranous portions
The left and right mainstem bronchi
questions.detail: This is the most common site for tracheal laceration.

questions.question: The "Macklin Effect" is a common cause of pneumomediastinum in trauma resulting from:
choices: Alveolar rupture with air dissecting along bronchovascular sheaths
questions.detail: This is the definition of the Macklin Effect.

questions.question: A patient presents with vomiting, chest pain, and "Hammond’s Crunch" on physical exam. What is the suspected diagnosis?
questions.detail: Ruptured esophagus from vomiting.

questions.question: What is the recommended "Next Step" for a patient with suspected Boerhaave Syndrome?
questions.detail: This is the definitive "Next Step".

questions.question: How is "Pneumopericardium" distinguished from "Pneumomediastinum" on a chest X-ray?
choices: Pneumopericardium air extends above the great vessels.
Pneumomediastinum air does NOT extend above the great vessels.
Pneumopericardium air is confined to the sac and does NOT extend above the great vessels.
There is no radiographic way to tell them apart.
questions.detail: Highly testable differentiator from pneumomediastinum.

questions.question: Which condition is defined as having 3 or more segmental fractures in a rib, or more than 5 adjacent rib fractures?
questions.detail: Defined by these rib fracture counts; causes paradoxical motion.

questions.question: A "Hemothorax" should be suspected in the setting of trauma if pleural fluid measures which Hounsfield Unit (HU) density?
questions.detail: This is the characteristic density of blood/hemothorax.

questions.question: In an upright patient, where does pleural air first accumulate in a pneumothorax?
questions.detail: Pleural air rises in the upright position.

questions.question: The "Deep Sulcus Sign" is a classic radiographic sign of a pneumothorax in which patient position?
questions.detail: The deep sulcus sign is the Supine appearance of a PTX.

questions.question: What is the emergency treatment for a tension pneumothorax?
questions.detail: Immediate treatment for tension PTX.

questions.question: An "Extrapleural Hematoma" is characterized on imaging by which classic finding?
choices: Air extending above the great vessels
Gas-fluid level in a lung cyst
questions.detail: This is the buzzword/sign for extrapleural hematoma.

questions.question: If a fluid collection persists after a pleural tube has been correctly placed, you should suspect:
questions.detail: Classic history is persistent fluid after tube placement.

questions.question: "Pulmonary Contusion" is the most common lung injury from blunt trauma and typically presents as:
choices: Non-segmental areas of consolidation with subpleural sparing
A round mass with a gas-fluid level
questions.detail: Alveolar hemorrhage appearance of contusion.

questions.question: What is the typical timeframe for a pulmonary contusion to appear and then disappear?
choices: Appears at 24 hours, disappears in 1 week
Appears within 6 hours, disappears within 72 hours
Appears after 72 hours, persists for months
Appears at birth, disappears by Day 3
questions.detail: Contusion resolution is rapid.

questions.question: A "Pulmonary Laceration" results in a tear in the lung that looks like a:
questions.detail: A lung tear results in a pneumatocele.

questions.question: A patient presents 1-2 days after a femur fracture with a clinical triad of rash, altered mental status, and shortness of breath. This is classic for:
questions.detail: Occurs 1-2 days post femur fracture with pulmonary edema appearance.

questions.question: Lungs with which underlying condition are "protected" from barotrauma during positive pressure ventilation because they don't stretch?
questions.detail: Lungs that don't stretch are protected from barotrauma.

questions.question: What is the most common radiographic appearance of a "malpositioned" chest tube?
choices: Tube in the right upper lobe bronchus
questions.detail: Most common malposition; results in air leaks.

questions.question: If you see hemopericardium in the setting of trauma, what should you suggest to the emergency department?
questions.detail: Suggest this if hemopericardium is seen in trauma.

questions.question: A "massive air leak" in the setting of trauma should make you think of:
questions.detail: Trauma + massive air leak = airway injury.

questions.question: A "hot PET scan" with activity around the periphery of a lesion is a strategy to identify:
questions.detail: Pulmonary infarcts are classically PET hot around the periphery.

questions.question: "Multi-lobar collapse" is a classic presentation for:
questions.detail: Multi-lobar collapse is a strategy buzzword for sarcoid.

questions.question: When you see "persistent fluid collection after pleural drain placement," the answer is:
questions.detail: Repeated from above, this is a "Sledgehammer" association.

questions.question: In the setting of trauma, the presence of a "ropy appearance" and hyperinflation should actually make you think of which neonatal condition if seen on a babygram?
questions.detail: "Ropy" = Meconium.

questions.question: "Linear lucencies" on a neonatal CXR in the setting of mechanical ventilation represent:
questions.detail: Linear lucencies in a ventilated neonate.

questions.question: What is the recommended treatment for an infant with PIE?
questions.detail: Brute force tactic for PIE.

questions.question: A "Big Fucking Mass" (B.F.M.) in the chest of a child under age 2 is most likely:
questions.detail: B.F.M. in child < 2.

questions.question: If the child with a B.F.M. is age 10+ and has an "eaten up rib," the diagnosis is:
questions.detail: B.F.M. in child age 10+ with rib invasion.

questions.question: On an expiratory film, a "lucency that stays lucent" (air trapping) is a positive sign for:
questions.detail: "Lucency side down" that stays lucent is a positive test.

questions.question: "Hammond’s Crunch" is associated with air in which location?
questions.detail: Hammond’s crunch is the physical sign of pneumomediastinum.

questions.question: A central line with an "abrupt bend" at the tip near the cavo-atrial junction should make you think it is in the:
questions.detail: An abrupt bend near the cavo-atrial junction suggests the azygos.

questions.question: If a central venous catheter is seen on the LEFT side of the heart, what are the two main possibilities?
choices: Arterial or in a duplicated SVC
It is always a normal variant
questions.detail: Standard locations for a left-sided central line.

questions.question: Central lines are a known risk factor for which vascular complication?
questions.detail: Central lines are a known risk factor.

questions.question: The "pseudo-lesion / hot quadrate sign" on CT is seen in which condition?
questions.detail: Hot quadrate sign is seen with SVC occlusion/syndrome.

questions.question: What is the ideal position for the tip of a Swan-Ganz catheter on a CXR?
choices: At the edge of the mediastinal shadow (proximal PA)
questions.detail: Ideal Swan-Ganz tip position.

questions.question: "Distal migration" of a Swan-Ganz catheter can result in:
questions.detail: Distal Swan-Ganz migration causes infarcts.

questions.question: "Proximal migration" (too far in/back) of a Swan-Ganz catheter can result in:
questions.detail: Proximal Swan-Ganz migration into the RV causes arrhythmia.

questions.question: An Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump (IABP) is used in cardiogenic shock for the purpose of:
questions.detail: IABP provides back pressure to improve perfusion.

questions.question: The correct location for an IABP balloon is in the proximal descending aorta, just BELOW the origin of the:
questions.detail: Correct IABP balloon location.

questions.question: On a CXR, the "ideal position" for an IABP tip is in the region of the:
questions.detail: The CXR landmark for ideal IABP tip position.

questions.question: A common complication of IABP malpositioning (too high) is:
choices: Obstruction of the left subclavian artery
questions.detail: Complication of IABP being too high.

questions.question: In the stages of CHF, "Stage 1: Redistribution" corresponds to a pulmonary capillary wedge pressure of:
questions.detail: Stage 1 CHF wedge pressure.

questions.question: "Kerley Lines and Peribronchial Cuffing" are features of which stage of CHF?
choices: It is seen in all stages
questions.detail: Associated with Kerley lines and a wedge pressure of 18-25.

questions.question: "Alveolar Edema" with airspace "fluffy" opacities and pleural effusions occurs when the wedge pressure is:
questions.detail: Stage 3 Alveolar Edema wedge pressure.

questions.question: The tip of an Endotracheal Tube (ETT) should ideally be how many centimeters from the carina?
questions.detail: Tip of ETT should be 5 cm from carina.

questions.question: Neck positioning affects ETT tip height. According to the mnemonic, "the hose goes where the...":
questions.detail: Neck flexion/extension causes tip to move with the nose.

questions.question: Intubation of which structure is the "most common goof" because of its more shallow angle?
questions.detail: Shallower angle makes this more common.

questions.question: "Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy Device" is the specific vocabulary word for which device?
questions.detail: Vocabulary for resynchronization.

questions.question: Left ventricular (LV) pacing leads reach their destination via which structure?
choices: The Coronary Sinus (to a posterior/lateral vein)
questions.detail: LV leads reach the posterior/lateral vein via the CS.

questions.question: On a lateral CXR, a correctly placed bi-ventricular LV lead should be seen pointing:
questions.detail: The LV lead should point posteriorly on lateral view.

questions.question: "Twiddler’s Syndrome" refers to:
choices: Malposition of a Swan-Ganz in the right ventricle
Lead displacement caused by the patient flipping/twisting the generator pack
Azygos bend in a central line
questions.detail: Mnemonic about social security checks.

questions.question: If a pacemaker lead looks like it is "poking through the wall" on a lateral CXR, what is the likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Lead "poking through the wall" on lateral CXR.

questions.question: Veno-Arterial (V-A) ECMO provides support for:
questions.detail: V-A ECMO supports both.

questions.question: In V-A ECMO, which vessels are typically "sacrificed" or used for catheter placement?
questions.detail: These are "sacrificed" for V-A ECMO.

questions.question: A "Lung White Out" appearance in a patient on ECMO is considered:
choices: A normal and expected finding (due to atelectasis/physiology)
questions.detail: "Lung white out" is normal on ECMO.

questions.question: A "crucial complication" for kids on ECMO, requiring routine head ultrasound screening, is:
questions.detail: Head ultrasounds are routine due to anticoagulation risk.

questions.question: In Veno-Venous (V-V) ECMO, the catheter position is typically:
questions.detail: Position for V-V lung support.

questions.question: The most common site for "arrhythmogenic foci" causing atrial fibrillation is the sleeve of which pulmonary vein?
questions.detail: Most common site for a-fib foci.

questions.question: If you see "hot clumps of signal in the lungs" on a Sulfur Colloid Liver-Spleen scan, the cause is:
choices: Too much Aluminum (Al) in the Technetium
questions.detail: Causes clumped signal on sulfur colloid scans.

questions.question: In the "CAVITY" mnemonic for lung cavities, the "V" stands for Vascular, which includes:
questions.detail: The "V" in the CAVITY mnemonic.
